speaker: interviewer
text: You were just talking about what it was like to tell other people of-- about your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. In '74, I think, I mentioned that I got this letter from my brother and then was preparing for the trip in '75.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you-- did you get the letter unexpectedly?

speaker: interviewee
text: The letter came through the Jewish Family Service-- somehow end up at a Jewish Family Service. It went through the Swiss Red Cross. My brother has written to the Swiss Red Cross, and it came to the United States. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sergei or Abraham?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham did that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He really search for me. He has written many letters before, and they-- a lot of them returned. He had the addresses, the old addresses of my aunt and uncle, and they all-- you know, they came back. So he did that. I was very happy, of course, to hear that they're both there. And I remember when I was preparing for the trip, at that time, it was-- you know, very few people traveled. '74, there's some Americans traveling to Russia, but not that many, and mostly with tours, and that's the only way I wanted to go. I feared the Russians. If something happens, I don't want to get stuck there. Because they could-- they were always suspicious of everything, and they could arrest you for anything. Well, when I-- they couldn't tell us exactly-- or they-- when and where we gonna be. So they told us, first, we gonna arrive to Moscow Airport. From Moscow we go to Leningrad. Of course, it didn't make sense, but that's what they said. So my brother's got a hotel in Leningrad, and they told me which hotel. And I-- so, when I get to New York, we-- we get on the airplane, they said, no, we gonna go stay in Moscow. We're not going to Leningrad. So here I begin the trip. My brother's already in Leningrad, and I have to stay four days in Moscow before I get to see it. It was terribly aggravating. It was so upsetting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days were you planning on being in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was like a eight-day trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So half of your time was basically wasted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So when I get to-- when I get to Moscow, I call the operator-- they said the operator speaks-- speaks English-- and I said I'm trying to contact the hotel in Leningrad-- I have the name of the hotel-- and my brothers are staying there. So I get the operator, and I get-- don't get anywhere with him. Can't even give the operator Leningrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I keep on calling half a day, and finally, we got the hotel in Leningrad, and they tell me that my brothers don't stay there. They are not there. And I'm-- I'm just going crazy. Here I am. I mean, all these years, I look forward, and my brothers-- I've traveled to my brothers, and I can't even contact them. So there was a lady with me who was from Washington DC, and she spoke fluent Russian. And we tried very hard. We had to go to a place where you make phone calls. It was like a telephone station, you know, thousands of people, everybody wants to make phone calls.

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't get it from the hotel room. For some reason, we just couldn't make good contact. We finally got a hold of the hotel-- I mean, the hotel operator, and I said-- and we-- we insisted. I said, they must be. They must be there. They're there. They told me they were there. Finally, he said, oh, yeah. It's another part of the building. So stupid. So my brothers were in the airport all day waiting for me in Leningrad. All day long, and I'm in Moscow. So if I-- I tell you, I just thought I'm gonna die that day. Well, 11 o'clock at night, my brother calls the hotel and gets a hold of me, and I talked to him. And I tell him that I have to stay in a hotel three days before I go to Leningrad. He said, well-- he said, OK. I mean, there's not much else-- I cannot go on my own. I'm part of a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the next morning, we have breakfast at a hotel. The waiter comes, and he says to me, while we're having breakfast, he says, Mr. Miller, the telephone for you. I said, oh, OK. He said, finish your-- I thought, doesn't even make sense. How can I finish, waiting for the telephone? So I-- I get to the-- this is an old hotel. And I get to the mezzanine, and I look up in the mezzanine, and there's two guys standing, my brothers, standing. They decided they're not gonna wait three days. They took the night train. It's an all-night from-- from Leningrad to Moscow, and they got here that morning. So here are the two brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you see, I have pictures of them when they were young, and all that. I did have some pictures from them. But those are the pictures were ingrained in my mind, and I remember him, and I see my brother is already 60 years old. And that was '75. He's-- yeah, 60 at that time, in '61. And-- and of course, they look at me, and they-- they-- they they never knew what I looked like. They've seen a picture. They thought I was taller. They were taller. I mean, I guess they didn't know what I went through. Or they did know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we got together, and it was just unbelievable, and we couldn't communicate. We couldn't talk to each other. I mean, really. You know. We started-- you know, stop, and you're looking for-- searching for an expression, for words. So we got a interpreter, and she helped us with the English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately, we're able to stay in that hotel. They had a room in the same hotel. As a rule, they never allowed tourists to stay with people, you know, with Russians. So they stayed. And we-- and after-- yeah, our Yiddish got a little bit better. We were able to communicating. I-- I studied a little Russian. They were able to go on the-- on the tours with me. So on they trip, which was really nice. So we spent seven, eight days together. I was not allowed to go to their city, the city [INAUDIBLE]. They made missiles, manufactured missiles. So that city was off-limits, I mean, for-- for tourists, for people from out of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was just a wonderful-- after we met, and we were together. And then '75, I think, in '85, I went again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I tried to-- wanted to come to this country, but they had to go indirectly, and they kept on putting off. And now we are too old, and, you know, learning the language, and all that new life. You wanted them to emigrate here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I wanted-- you know. At that time, they were not really-- maybe they were thinking about it, but they just felt it's too late in life to do that. My older brother said that. And then-- '85-- in '91, my brother was very ill, my second-- my second older brother. So at that time, I was allowed to go to his city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to [INAUDIBLE], and I stayed with him for two weeks. And-- and he was-- you know, they didn't think he's gonna make it. He had heart problem. He had-- he was diabetic. And they had no medication for him. They just-- in Russia they just had none of that. Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Abraham, [? Misha. ?] When I-- when I was there with him, he says, you are my best medicine. I was-- so-- that made me feel good, and-- and he felt better, and we had such a good time for two weeks. We were able to talk about different things. He was an easy guy to communicate. He was somewhat different than my older brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My older brother is a thinker. He was a-- he read all his life. And-- and, you know, to him, he's got to make perfect sense when he talks. This guy is a lot like-- he'll just let it come out. And we had a wonderful time for two weeks. And that was '91, and he died in '90-- in '92, with all these complications he had. And he was gonna come. He had papers to come here. He has one daughter, my niece, and-- and she had two children. They are in this country now. I brought them here. And they bought a house in Dublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dublin, Ohio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they've been here for-- it's gonna be four years in April. They're doing fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- did both of your brothers marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my other brother, the oldest brother, never married. And he went-- he decided he was gonna go to Israel, because there was a large Russian-Jewish community. And all-- he was interested in are Russia library, I mean, you know, if he has access to that, that would be just fine, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- I visited Israel in '92, and two years ago, he came here for a couple weeks. And I'm gonna go again, this coming February, to see him. He's 76 years old. He had bypass surgery last year. Your brother Abraham's wife? Did she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was his second wife. He was married for about three years. No, she's in Russia. She's just in the Ukraine. His first wife died of cancer. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You talked about training for a marathon. You've been a runner for a number of years. And I'm just sort of struck by the-- the parallel of, during the war, you spend most of your time running very fast for long periods of time. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you do this as a choice. Is there a connection?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I-- I said I was a fugitive. I've been running all my life. I-- I guess they're both-- I was running for life then, and I'm running for life now. I think we started doing that for, you know, people who were dying of heart diseases and all that in-- in the late '60s. Actually, the group that I started jogging with were older than I. I end up running with some of their sons now. I've been-- you know-- so, I guess, running for health in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're active in other sports too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took up golf about six years ago. Since all the years I worked weekends, then I had-- had time. So-- so now I have time, and I enjoy the game. Frustrated, but it's enjoyable. I'm an outdoor man. I love to be on the outside. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And both of your children are grown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My son is 28, and 27, and my daughter is 24. My son is working with computers. My daughter still has some problems, trying to find-- find herself. She's been a beautiful child. She's been a good kid, but she had some problems. And that, they're both in Columbus, and I get to see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a long career with Lazarus?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, yes. I worked 37 years at Lazarus, a place where I was gonna stay six months. And-- and-- I'm in good health. I feel good, and life is-- is OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife, you and your wife are divorced?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been divorced for 12 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we sort of bring your story to a conclusion, is there anything that we skipped over, when we talked about all your different times running and surviving? Is there anything that you might want to make sure gets in the story? Well, I'm sure there's a lot of things skipped over, but how can you tell your three years and every day with experiences? But you heard most things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, it's difficult, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] without having notes to go through all the days and weeks and experiences I've had. I'm sure there's things we missed, a lot of things we've missed. But-- Are you still an observant Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still an observant Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm observant, but not-- I go to orthodox synagogue. I'm not orthodox, but I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm a Jew, all the way. I-- I'm a Jew in-- in my own way. I belong to a community. I belong to a synagogue. I-- I support all-- a lot of charities and my synagogue, and-- and in that way, I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple of times when you were telling your story, you talked about having such a narrow escape, that you must have been, you know, saying the words of God, or God must have been right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- Do you have a faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: I struggle with that. I mean, there were times where I felt-- you know, when I said to you, like God fed me with the right words to say, and probably saved my life, maybe more than once. But then again, I wonder, where was God when-- when my innocent mother and my brothers, and little brother, was being killed? I-- you know, so I struggle with that. I-- I struggle with that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel there is a God, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but at times you wonder where he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: You prepared a sort of a statement that you'd like to read on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I [? worked with ?] that. I just wonder if it's-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, I said in the Holocaust, I lost my parents, two brothers, and a younger sister. One of the brothers was only three and a half years old. But I did not lose my humanity. I was bitter, but not consumed by hatred, not the kind of hatred that eats at you, so to exclude everything else. Alone at the age of 12 through the age of 15, I was running and hiding constantly, one step ahead of death, always, dead of the hands of these Ukrainians. They had no regard of human lives. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Always engraved in my memory was my mother's prayer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of us must survive. One of us must remain. I had to survive the hardship, and hope by my survival and my message contained in this interview, may in some small measure, a very small measure, forestall another Holocaust, another Holocaust to destroy humanity and people. I'm hoping that my message will bring some hope that we can live as humans together. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We are looking at.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture of the little-- the shtetl, Targowica. In Yiddish, we would call it [YIDDISH]. There's the river Ikva. There's a bridge right over here. And this is the house where I was born and lived with my family. On the top of the hill is my grandparents' house, right over here, and right in back was my uncle's house. This is a close-up that my brother drew out of that picture. So there's the house and my grandparents' and my uncle's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother who lives in Israel. My brother Israel [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he draw this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: He drew that picture about when he came to Israel, four years ago. He used to complain he has arthritis, which he does, and I always asked him to paint something for me, because I know how good he was. And he says, I cannot do it. Well, that was a pleasant surprise, that he did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a synagogue in Targowica. And the people here are gathered when our rabbi died. This was right before the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He died in another little town. It's about 15 miles. That's where the doctor was. And they carried him for 15 miles in one of these heavy caskets all way to-- to [INAUDIBLE], and this was the time of the funeral. And it's interesting about this synagogue, was very old, was-- I was told it was over 100 years old when I was a kid. This synagogue was built, but there were no nails. There were wooden pegs. And-- I-- then when they killed the Jews from that town, they sold the synagogue to a farmer's for the lumber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I happen to have been to this farmer where I worked, and I saw the Aron Kodesh, you know, the-- where they keep the Torahs. The-- the-- his neighbor bought it. And he asked him, he says, what do you think you gonna do with that? He says, I don't know. He says, I may make a toilet out of it. I don't know if it's important to bring it up, but that's-- I had to say it. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pull-- pull it. Do a full shot with-- with this one. He wants the-- the whole scene in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is an interesting picture. I was always fascinated--

speaker: interviewer
text: This isn't on yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I think, gives you a little bit of history of my little shtetl. You could see, this is the guy, the-- the-- the-- the-- that it was the-- not the cab driver, but the [YIDDISH] in Yiddish. My aunt was visiting us from the United States, and she is on her way to Lutsk. So before she-- and there's my uncle, there's my brother. And there was my American aunt, and there's another one of my oldest brothers right here, on her way to Lutsk, which was only about 15, 16 miles, and from there she'll take the train. But this was the means of transportation from-- from our shtetl to the next bigger city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not in it. I-- I don't think I'm in it. But-- no, my brothers. This is my brother on this horse, and this is the barn that was part of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my third older brother, Zindel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when would this picture have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, probably, in 1937. God, that's a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my family at the time when my aunt from Columbus, Ohio was visiting us in Europe. My aunt is to the right, over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is [? Jean ?] Gelin, at that time Pressman. It was before she was married. And this is the family. That's me. And, of course, my dad and my brothers and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's next to your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- that was Zindel, and that was [INAUDIBLE], and [? Abram, ?] Abraham. And that's my aunt, [? Jean, ?] and my sister, Rose. My mother. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was me. Yeah, I think-- OK, the back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is dated.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the picture is dated. It's [NON-ENGLISH]. This is, my mother sent it to her sister, Chaja, who's not in this picture. She died Majdanek. I never got to see her. And it says, 'from the family of Millers.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's dated November 22?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's dated, yes. 29-- yeah. November the 20-- the 22nd of November, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get a hold of these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt had all these pictures. Frankly, I-- I haven't brought anything with me from home. I-- I had nothing. But she had all these pictures that the family through the years been sending pictures, and-- so that's how I got them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Uncle Meir and my Aunt Perl, and her son-- their son-- Einikh, the taller one, and my smaller, younger cousin, Moishe, and that's the same-- my aunt from Columbus, Ohio, Aunt [? Jean. ?] This, too, was taken November the 22nd, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did not survive the Holocaust, in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my aunt and uncle died, and my older cousin, he survived the Holocaust. He was in the partisans. And then he volunteered into the Russian army so he could take revenge on the Germans, and he did. And I got a letter from him. He was telling me what he was doing. And somehow he didn't make it. He was shot in-- on the front in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my aunt and uncle are not living. And this is-- my aunt-- this is my aunt died in Columbus, Ohio in 1974. She-- she was from the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's brother, Uncle Icek. And-- and Chaja. They lived in a small village not far from us, and they too were killed in the Holocaust, in [INAUDIBLE] in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture would have been taken about--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '36, '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a Uncle Meir. This was taken in the early '30s. Uncle Meir was my mother's brother, and it was taken in Lutsk, which is the next largest city to Targowica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never-- yeah, he did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me know when.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandparents who lived in Columbus, Ohio, which I've never met. And they-- my-- my grandfather died before I was born. That picture, my grandmother and their three cousins. And one of them is Betty Jean and-- and Loretta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in-- in Columbus, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken-- well, I would say in the-- in the early-- in-- in-- maybe, in-- in '28. '27, '28.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my younger sister, Rose, and this was taken when she was about, probably, five years old. And this was in '37, 1937. This picture was taken right after I was liberated by the Russians. This is taken in Rovno, when I found out that one of my brothers was in Russia, alive. So I've taken that picture and mailed it to him, and he kept it all the years until I seen him for the first time in '75. He-- he showed me that picture. I always wanted to see that picture. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who took it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a photographer, a professional photographer, they-- taking that picture. And this was-- I was dressed in a overmade old Russian army overcoat, and everything else was from-- from uniform, made to fit me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
